title: Google publishes two AI-agent user-agent tokens (Google-Extended; Google-CloudVertexBot) that assert AI-system identity in server logs but carry no principal, authority scope, or delegation record
primary_source_id: Google Search Central — Google common crawlers documentation
ai_system_type: LLM-backed web agent (Google Gemini AI Mode; Google Vertex AI agents)
impact_surface: web server access logs; publisher content governance; operator accountability
severity: Medium — no direct user harm; systemic gap in accountability infrastructure for AI-delegated web access
mitigation_or_response: Google-Extended lets publishers opt out of AI training use via robots.txt; Google-CloudVertexBot lets publishers block Vertex AI agent crawls. Neither token provides the authorising principal (which user or operator), authority scope, human approval record, or verifiable outcome attestation.
paper_use_note: Use to show that production-scale AI-agent infrastructure already implements identity signals (UA tokens) while the delegation-accountability layer (principal, mandate, approval, outcome) is absent. The gap between a UA assertion and an evidence-package attestation is the design point. Source is authoritative (official Google developer documentation). Do not claim user harm, data breach, or legal violation.
verification_gap: NONE — verified 2026-06-21 against official Google Search Central documentation. "Google-Agent" in media reports is not an official UA string; the authoritative tokens are Google-Extended and Google-CloudVertexBot.
